title: Device bearers use versioned domain-separated verifiers
context: ADR-0009 established an adapter-neutral device credential lifecycle and ADR-0010 established sealed credential persistence, but neither defined the production entropy source or sealed-secret representation. SDD-007 asked how the self-hosted server could verify possession of a device bearer without persisting a usable token and without treating a high-entropy machine credential like a human password. Node.js documents randomBytes as cryptographically strong pseudorandom data and timingSafeEqual as a constant-time byte comparison whose surrounding code must still avoid leakage. RFC 7636 recommends 32 random octets encoded as 43 base64url characters and distinguishes that high-entropy verifier from password material. Slice 1 generated exactly 32 random bytes, sealed the complete production bearer with a versioned domain-separated SHA-256 digest, parsed verifiers strictly before comparing equal 32-byte values, and failed issuance or authentication closed on malformed inputs and primitive failures. Tests demonstrated exact entropy shape, domain input, plaintext exclusion, token and verifier substitutions, constant-time comparison shape, lifecycle composition, rotation invalidation, and no store mutation after failed sealing. The human authority accepted H-007 only within that production-codec boundary.
decision: The self-hosted server generates each device credential secret from exactly 32 bytes returned by Node.js randomBytes and encodes those bytes as an unpadded 43-character base64url value. It generates a protocol-valid opaque credential identifier with Node.js randomUUID. The complete bearer is accepted for sealing only when it matches the production device-token grammar. The stored verifier is `okdv1$sha256$<digest>`, where `<digest>` is the canonical unpadded base64url encoding of SHA-256 over the UTF-8 bytes of `overlaykit:device-credential:v1`, followed by one zero byte and then the complete bearer bytes. Authentication accepts only that exact version, algorithm, canonical 32-byte digest, and production token grammar; it computes the candidate in the same domain and compares two equal-length 32-byte values with timingSafeEqual. Entropy or digest failures abort issuance before storage, while malformed verifiers, unsupported versions or algorithms, digest failures, and comparison failures deny authentication. This verifier is for a 256-bit machine-generated secret and must not replace the password KDF used for human credentials.
consequences: A persisted device credential record contains a versioned verifier and never the bearer plaintext or a decryption key.
A verifier copied from storage is not syntactically accepted as an OverlayKit device bearer.
Verifier version and algorithm are explicit so unsupported representations fail closed and future migrations require a separate decision.
Domain separation binds this digest representation to OverlayKit device credentials and prevents accidental reuse as another token class verifier.
No salt or slow password KDF is added to this machine credential; its offline guessing resistance depends on successful generation of the full 256-bit random secret and SHA-256 preimage resistance.
The server adapter remains structurally compatible with the source-published protocol lifecycle without adding a production runtime import across the current CommonJS and ESM package boundary.

Cryptographic primitive correctness, runtime certification, memory secrecy, host compromise, transport, bearer disclosure, replay, rate limiting, endpoint binding, in-flight revocation, multi-process storage, and hardware integration remain separate questions.

policies.statement: A production device secret must be generated from exactly 32 bytes returned by the approved cryptographic random source and encoded as canonical unpadded base64url.
A stored device verifier must use the accepted version and algorithm and hash the complete bearer after the device-credential domain separator.
Authentication must reject unsupported or noncanonical verifier representations before comparing an equal-length 32-byte candidate and stored digest with the approved constant-time primitive.
Entropy or sealing failure prevents credential persistence, and verifier parsing, digest, or comparison failure denies authentication.
The device verifier construction applies only to a 256-bit machine-generated secret and may not replace the approved KDF for human passwords.

Passing adapter tests may not be presented as primitive certification, memory or host compromise resistance, transport security, bearer recovery, replay prevention, rate limiting, endpoint integration, or hardware support.
